segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an exterior shot of a Five Guys restaurant in a European city, likely Paris, featuring red awnings and outdoor seating, accompanied by upbeat, slightly jazzy background music. A female narrator states, "American fast food is taking over France." The scene transitions to patrons eating inside Five Guys, then to modern storefronts of Popeyes and Krispy Kreme, which the narrator identifies as "recent arrivals." A young man with an afro and sunglasses stands before a McDonald’s adorned with yellow flowers, as a male voice with a French accent remarks, "We appreciate more American fast food than Americans themselves." This is followed by a KFC storefront, and another male voice with a French accent declares, "KFC, of course, because I love chicken." A woman eating at a Five Guys counter, wearing a black hoodie with 'TRODHE', is seen as a female voice with a French accent describes fast food as "the hype," "the trend now," and "addictive," bringing "happiness to people." The video cuts to a bustling street scene with fast food outlets, overlaid with text revealing that "1,300 fast food stores opened in France 2019-2023" and generated "€19b" in revenue, with the narrator confirming these figures. A gourmet dish is shown, contrasting with the fast food theme, as the narrator explains that "the French palate is changing" despite the country's reputation for gastronomy, adding that "France is McDonald's and Burger King's second largest market outside of the US." A McDonald’s is then shown at dusk with people entering and exiting, and a man eating a burger inside. A laptop screen displays an interview with Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, who, with a French accent, explains that "a lot of brands want to come in France because France is an Eldorado. You do two or three times sales more than the other country." The segment concludes with a woman walking out of a KFC holding a branded bag, followed by a wide view of a cobblestone street where the woman in a pink coat, still carrying her KFC bag, walks past a souvenir kiosk and a café, as a final male voice with an American accent briefly mentions a "country that has a high pro..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, speaking in a professional indoor setting, wearing a headset and suit, discussing the success of American brands and demographics in France. A female narrator then questions how American fast-food companies won over the "culinary conscious, fine dining, Michelin star-loving French crowd," stating that CNBC visited Paris to investigate. Upbeat, light background music with a French feel begins, accompanying a transition to a bustling Parisian street scene. We see a Burger King with a red awning and outdoor seating, where pedestrians, including a person in a yellow jacket, walk by. The camera then captures a busy cobblestone street lined with shops and cafes, including a Heineken-branded café, as people walk in both directions, with ambient street sounds audible. The narrator identifies this as lunchtime in the Les Halles neighborhood. A title card, 'Why the French love American fast food,' appears, followed by a scenic view of the Seine River with runners and historic buildings. Over this, a male interviewee, speaking French with an accent, explains his preference for fries and nuggets. The video then shows a McDonald’s restaurant with yellow flowers above the entrance, where a man in a green jacket and glasses stands holding a McDonald’s bag. A close-up of a McDonald’s burger appears with a text overlay: '61% increase in fast-food sales in France 2013-2023,' as the narrator confirms this significant rise. A pie chart follows, detailing American fast-food market shares in France for 2023, with McDonald’s leading at 34.5%, Burger King at 9.1%, KFC at 4.1%, Domino’s Pizza at 1.9%, Subway at 1.2%, and 'Others' at 49.1%, while the narrator highlights McDonald's and Burger King as major players with combined sales of 8.3 billion euros. The scene shifts to a Quick restaurant with a red sign and burger logo, accompanied by a text overlay stating '47% fast-food sales in France from burgers in 2023,' with the narrator emphasizing the big role burgers play. The overall atmosphere transitions from professional interview to vibrant urban life, using visual and statistical evidence to underscore the popularity of American fast food in France, all set to the continuous background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens on a bustling cobblestone street in Paris, featuring a Quick restaurant, with a yellow text overlay stating '47% fast-food sales in France from burgers in 2023', as a female narrator explains that burgers accounted for about half of the country's sales that year. The scene transitions to a laptop screen where Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, speaking with a French accent, asserts that "the burger has taken a growing share of the market over the last 30 years and has become an essential item on every restaurant menu." This is followed by a close-up of people eating burgers, including a woman with curly hair and glasses taking a bite, as the narrator adds, "But it's not only burgers." A vintage television then displays a grainy McDonald’s commercial, while the narrator explains that "American fast-food companies, helped by clever marketing, built their brands behind iconic products." The view cuts to a KFC storefront on a Parisian street, with a woman inside eating, as the male speaker with a French accent continues, "People in France want to discover other tastes and when you explain that with a good story with an iconic burger, you succeed," before listing iconic items like "Popeyes is a chicken sandwich. For McDonald's it's a Big Mac. For Burger King it's a Whopper." A close-up of two large burgers with sesame seed buns, clearly from Burger King packaging, accompanies this commentary. Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, then appears speaking indoors, explaining in clear English that despite France being "the country of the Michelin restaurant" with "gastronomy, very high standards," QSR fast food is "very trendy," showing that "even though we appreciate food, we still understand that we love treating ourselves." This commentary accompanies a hand placing a Domino’s pizza box on a table, a close-up of a Domino’s pizza being sliced, a man eating inside a KFC, and a woman exiting a McDonald’s holding a drink. The segment concludes with an animated bar chart titled 'American fast-food companies in France by profit 2018', displaying outlet numbers for McDonald’s, Burger King, KFC, Domino’s, and Subway, as the female narrator begins to discuss "the country's top performing..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a nighttime exterior of a Popeyes restaurant, its vibrant red neon sign and fried chicken graphic reflected in large glass windows, as a man eats inside and a bicycle is parked outside. A female narrator explains how American quick-service restaurants (QSRs) took a specialized approach when entering the French market. The scene shifts to a bustling daytime outdoor café in a European city, then to Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, in a dark suit and headset, speaking in a professional indoor setting. He discusses the cliché of France being a fancier place to eat, noting how QSR concepts focused on modernized images, higher quality, higher price points, and more innovative offerings. The interior of a Five Guys restaurant with red and white checkered walls and employees serving milkshakes is shown, followed by an aerial view of a combine harvester in a golden wheat field and a close-up of a potato harvester loading potatoes onto a truck. An indoor poultry farm with thousands of chicks under automated feeders appears. The narrator highlights that this includes sourcing ingredients from French farmers, like the potatoes used for McDonald's French fries and the chicken in Popeyes' sandwiches. Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, is seen on a laptop screen with a CNBC mug, speaking about how French people "don't get fed, we eat" and treat food with emotion, emphasizing the need for American brands to bring emotion and connection. He further stresses the importance of building strong partnerships with farmers for QSR American brands to succeed. The video then shows a KFC restaurant exterior in Paris with its Colonel Sanders sign, a modern McDonald’s building, and a close-up of McDonald’s fries. The segment concludes with a vintage CRT television displaying a grainy, flickering McDonald’s commercial, as the narrator states that American QSRs have adapted to European Union food standards, resulting in successful products.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an exterior shot of a modern McDonald’s restaurant in France, showing customers dining through large glass windows, followed by a close-up of a card payment for a McDonald's meal. The scene then displays four gourmet-style square pizzas, suggesting menu adaptations as a female narrator explains how brands cater to French palates by altering menu items or creating new products. Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, is then seen in an office setting, speaking with a French accent about the importance of being 'French friendly' and highlighting their 'raclette' campaign as their most successful. This is visually reinforced by a promotional image of Domino’s Raclette pizza and a split-screen TikTok montage showcasing its preparation and consumption with French text overlays like 'Authentique RACLETTE' and 'Ca sent TROP bon!'. The narrator notes these adaptations have paid off, with consumers now considering chains as much French as American. A male speaker with an accent, amidst background street noise, echoes this sentiment, stating that American brands like McDonald's and KFC feel like 'part of us now' and are 'everywhere.' The video shows people queuing outside a Parisian McDonald’s and a couple walking past a Krispy Kreme storefront. A historical note appears, marking '50 years ago: In 1972, the first McDonald’s in France opened in Créteil,' over a roadside billboard. The narrator then explains McDonald's has the strongest foothold due to its first-mover advantage, as Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, clearly states that McDonald's was the 'first quick-service concept to go to France and found tremendous success there,' while speaking in a professional setting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an animated blue Burger King restaurant, transitioning to a torn-paper effect revealing a Los Angeles Times article about Burger King's expansion into France. A female narrator explains that while many have tried to replicate success, Burger King re-entered France after a 15-year hiatus in 2012, now boasting the second-highest number of American QSR franchises, a fact reinforced by a bar chart titled 'American fast-food restaurants in France' highlighting Burger King's 502 locations. The scene shifts to a blue animated map of France, where the narrator details the entry years of other chains: Pizza Hut in 1987, KFC in 1991, Subway in 2001, and Domino’s in 2006, noting their rapid expansion in the past decade. The narrator then mentions Popeyes and Krispy Kreme opening their first French stores in 2023 and 2024, as the video shows a real-world Krispy Kreme storefront and a woman in a yellow coat eating a red-frosted donut inside. A female voice with a French accent expresses enthusiasm, stating these are 'new brands in France' and 'very, very fantastic for the French people.' The video then shows a McDonald’s self-service kiosk with a hand interacting with the touchscreen displaying 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER,' followed by Xavier Expilly, President and Founder of EXPM, speaking on a laptop screen. The narrator explains that shifts in French society like digitization have allowed the fast-food industry to flourish. Expilly, speaking with a French accent, elaborates on these changes, mentioning 'takeaway, drive-thru, pick and collect, delivery,' and how 'all the QSR restaurant can do that,' calling it a 'perfect match for today needs with the hybridization of concepts, accelerating.' This aligns with visuals of delivery cyclists with teal thermal bags and a McDonald’s drive-thru sign reading 'COMMANDEZ ICI' with cars lined up.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a hand passing a white plastic bag through a car window, suggesting a drive-thru transaction, with a blurred car and taillight in the foreground. The scene transitions to the exterior of a Domino’s Pizza restaurant in France, where customers are entering and exiting, and blue delivery scooters are parked outside; the signage includes 'A emporter' (To go) and 'En livraison' (Delivery). Inside, Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, is interviewed, wearing a dark suit and white shirt, seated in a professional setting with a window behind him, explaining in a French accent that only "less than 10% of our orders" are at the counter, with "60% are online." A female narrator then highlights that "One in five meals in France is eaten outside the home," with experts predicting an increase. The camera then shows a customer placing a pizza box on a counter inside the Domino’s, with the brand’s logo on a black-tiled wall, followed by a woman with braided hair and glasses eating at a table inside the restaurant. Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, is then seen speaking in a professional indoor setting, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and headset, discussing in an American accent a "young population, high immigration population," and a "high GDP per capita country that leads to strong unit economics." The narrator adds that "America's pop culture influence is another powerful tool." A close-up follows of a 5 Euro note and a receipt on a wooden table, with a glass of water beside it. The video then cuts to a street scene in Paris with cobblestone pavement, where pedestrians walk past a bar with a French menu. The camera then focuses on a woman in a gray coat and patterned scarf standing in front of a Burger King restaurant, speaking and gesturing with a French accent about how young people grew up watching American series and movies, eating fast food, and were "pretty excited" when fast food came to France. The narrator then notes that "Bigger portions and modest presentations appeal to even the most delicate palate," and that "High inflation and shortened lunch breaks have contributed to a need for convenience." The scene transitions to the bustling Burger King interior, where customers are seen entering, and two women are seated at a table, one with curly hair and a green shirt eating a burger and fries, while the other, wearing a pink scarf, eats a burger. Joel Tissier's voice returns, describing American food as "tasty, big."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a woman wearing a green and white patterned headscarf and a green face mask, standing in a protest crowd with a Palestinian flag visible, as a narrator discusses the "high Muslim population." The scene transitions to a wider shot of a Parisian street, showing a protester waving a Palestinian flag at a traffic light amidst other demonstrators holding signs like 'URGENCE PALESTINE', while the narration continues, highlighting France's large Muslim population. The camera then cuts to a McDonald’s storefront, defaced with graffiti and protest signs like 'BOYCOTT ISRAEL', as a graphic overlay confirms "7% France’s share of McDonald’s total revenue," aligning with the narrator's statement about France's contribution to the fast-food chain's revenue. Inside the McDonald’s, a customer interacts with a self-service kiosk displaying 'TOUCHEZ POUR COMMANDER', while the narrator explains McDonald's blamed weaker performance on "pricing backlash," acknowledging a sales drop in the US. The scene shifts to a Subway restaurant exterior with its iconic green and yellow sign, as a male voice with a French accent comments on "stringflation," noting how "they reduce the size of the burger and they raise the price." The narrator then reveals that "Subway closed more than 100 of its French storefronts since 2015," while the Subway exterior remains visible. Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, is then seen speaking in a professional setting, explaining that "the more near-term risk is going to be more macro-oriented that France seems to be the first European market that's really succumbing to restaurant industry traffic and restaurant industry traffic softness." The video returns to the McDonald’s exterior, showing pedestrians and then inside where customers use self-service kiosks displaying 'VOS BURGERS PRÉFÉRÉS, ÉGALEMENT DISPONIBLES AVEC DU BACON', as the narrator comments on the trickiness of "staying competitive in the new digital age." Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, is then shown speaking indoors, stating, "We come from a world where before 2016, the only people that were delivering were pizzas and sushis. I compete with pizzas on one hand, so I need to be..." This is followed by a shot of stacked Domino’s pizza boxes with the slogan 'TAKE A FRESH LOOK'.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a pizza topped with pepperoni, cheese, and green peppers, followed by a Domino’s Pizza box being opened, revealing the brand’s logo. Joel Tissier, CEO of Domino’s Pizza France, is then seen speaking indoors, his professional male voice explaining Domino's strategy to be "best in class" in the delivery business, acknowledging competitors like McDonald's and BK are "seven and twelve times bigger" than them. The scene shifts to an exterior shot of a Burger King restaurant in Paris, with pedestrians and a Théâtre du Caveau de la République sign. A female narrator's voice details how not everyone welcomes these expansions, with a protest sign against McDonald’s in Montorgueil ('Non au fast food géant dans la rue Montorgueil!') and a Change.org petition against a Burger King in Place Martin Nadaud, Paris 20, displayed, showing 24,932 signatures and a quote from Collectif Place Martin Nadaud: 'No to the promotion of outdated environmental practices'. The narrator continues, explaining that France implemented new laws in the same year, targeting fast-food companies over waste, requiring QSRs to offer reusable cutlery and cut down on single-use packaging. A Guardian article headline 'Ban on single-use restaurant tableware hailed as fast-food ‘revolution’ in France' appears, followed by scenes of people eating fast food at outdoor tables, including a woman with curly hair and glasses at a Burger King, and another person at a Five Guys restaurant. A different male voice, likely an industry expert, discusses creating "dishes especially for the QSR brown restaurants" and how "sustainability seems better than the others." Upbeat, light background music begins as the video ends with a shot of a Five Guys restaurant interior, where customers are ordering milkshakes and employees in red shirts are working behind the counter, while the female narrator concludes that "The QSR market in France has a lot more room to grow still," mentioning "Chipotle, for example."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with exterior shots of American fast-food chains in France, beginning with a Chipotle Mexican Grill storefront, which a female narrator notes "has only six locations so far." This transitions to a Taco Bell, which the narrator states "has not entered at all." A close-up then shows a gourmet dish being prepared, with a metal dome lifted to reveal seasoned meat on a tortilla, as a male voice with a French accent mentions potential menu items like "a burger, a beef, a chicken, and maybe the tacos in the future." Next, a Steak 'n Shake restaurant is shown with patrons dining outdoors, with the narrator adding it "now boasts 37 locations." A Carl’s Jr. location, marked 'OUVERTURE LE 18 JANVIER' (Opening January 18), appears, with the narrator explaining it "is playing catch-up after breaking ground on its first French location in 2023." A Wendy’s sign is briefly seen, with the narrator stating it "is supposedly planning a move too." The scene cuts to Andrew Charles, Managing Director at TD Cowen, in an interview, discussing how "the next push is going to be more of a focus on chicken," aiming for "higher margin" and a "younger consumer focused" strategy. This is followed by a Domino’s Pizza storefront and an employee placing a pizza box, while a male voice with a French accent discusses making their product "broadly available," having "close to 500 stores," and mentioning "the pizza distributor." The narrator then projects the industry to be worth "about 21.5 billion euros by 2028," concluding that "the French palate seems ready for it." The video ends with a KFC meal being carried and customers eating, including a woman in a green shirt and another in a pink scarf, as a male voice with a French accent remarks, "The taste is really different."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with exterior shots of American fast-food chains in France, beginning with a Chipotle Mexican Grill storefront featuring its red and black circular logo and a beige facade. The scene shifts to a close-up of a gourmet dish being prepared, where a metal dome is lifted to reveal a mound of seasoned meat on a tortilla, suggesting a high-end culinary presentation. Next, a Steak 'n Shake restaurant is shown with patrons dining outdoors under white umbrellas, seated at red tables, with a menu stand visible. The video then cuts to a Carl’s Jr. location, marked by a large yellow star logo and a sign reading 'OUVERTURE LE 18 JANVIER' (Opening January 18), with customers entering and others seated at outdoor tables. A Wendy’s sign with its iconic red-haired girl logo appears briefly. The scene transitions to an interview with a man in a dark suit and headset, seated in a well-lit room with white wainscoting and windows, speaking directly to the camera. This is followed by a shot of a Domino’s Pizza storefront with its red and blue logo, then a Domino’s employee placing a pizza box labeled 'MEDIUM' on a counter. The video then shows a red tray being carried with a KFC meal, including fries, a burger, and a drink, followed by a close-up of a customer at a table eating a KFC meal with orange juice, wearing a green shirt and white jacket, while another person in a pink scarf eats beside them. The final shot shows the same two individuals continuing to eat and drink, with the man gesturing animatedly as they interact.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a KFC restaurant with a close-up of two individuals enjoying a meal, accompanied by a light, upbeat background music. One person, wearing a green shirt with 'MNMM SINCE 2010' and a white jacket, eats fried chicken, while the other, in a dark jacket and pink scarf, sips orange juice. A male voice with an accent comments, "in front of uh the other restaurants. I think uh they have a special touch and that changes everything," as the red tray between them displays KFC-branded packaging, fries, and a burger. The scene then cuts to the person in the green shirt and white jacket, now sitting alone at an outdoor table in front of the KFC storefront, which advertises 'THE PLUS CHICKEN BURGER OF KFC'. The camera focuses on a red tray with a KFC meal as hands reach in to eat, with the background music continuing. The video transitions to the CNBC logo, followed by end credits on a white background listing production details including 'Produced and Edited by Natalie Rice', 'Camera by Federico Campanale', and 'Animation by Christina Locopo'. The final frames display the CNBC logo again with a call to action: 'Let us know what you think in the comments below', alongside 'Subscribe' and 'FIRST IN BUSINESS WORLDWIDE,' against a dark blue cityscape backdrop. The segment, published in March 2024, includes additional footage from various sources like Getty, AP Photos, Reuters, Domino’s Pizza, Chipotle, TikTok, IBISWorld, and McDonald’s.
